section.title: Laws of Restitution and Justice
section.sectionRationale: This chapter continues the Book of the Covenant's case law into property, negligence, sexual ethics, and social justice, closing with some of the Torah's most tender commands: do not oppress the foreigner, the widow, or the orphan, for the LORD Himself hears their cry.
section.thesis: The LORD legislates fair restitution for theft and property damage, protects the vulnerable, the foreigner, widow, orphan, and poor, from exploitation, and calls Israel to be a holy people whose ordinary economic and social life reflects the character of the God who redeemed them from Egypt.
section.frameNote: Five movements: laws of theft and restitution; property damage and disputes over safekeeping; sexual ethics and capital offenses against exclusive worship; tender protections for the foreigner, widow, orphan, and poor; and closing instructions on holiness in ordinary obligations.

verses.text: “If a man steals an ox or a sheep and slaughters or sells it, he must {{repay|repay}} five oxen for an ox and four sheep for a sheep.
If a thief is caught breaking in and is beaten to death, no one shall be guilty of bloodshed.

But if it happens after sunrise, there is guilt for his bloodshed. 
A thief must make full restitution; if he has nothing, he himself shall be sold for his theft.
If what was stolen is actually found alive in his possession, whether ox or donkey or sheep, he must pay back double.
If a man grazes his livestock in a field or vineyard and allows them to stray so that they graze in someone else's field, he must make restitution from the best of his own field or vineyard.
If a fire breaks out and spreads to thornbushes so that it consumes stacked or standing grain, or the whole field, the one who started the fire must make full restitution.
If a man gives his neighbor money or goods for safekeeping and they are stolen from the neighbor's house, the thief, if caught, must pay back double.
If the thief is not found, the owner of the house must appear before the judges to determine whether he has taken his neighbor's property.

In all cases of illegal possession of an ox, a donkey, a sheep, a garment, or any lost item that someone claims, ‘This is mine,’ both parties shall bring their cases before the judges. The one whom the judges find guilty must pay back double to his neighbor.
If a man gives a donkey, an ox, a sheep, or any other animal to be cared for by his neighbor, but it dies or is injured or stolen while no one is watching,
an oath before the LORD shall be made between the parties to determine whether or not the man has taken his neighbor's property. The owner must accept the oath and require no restitution.
But if the animal was actually stolen from the neighbor, he must make restitution to the owner.
If the animal was torn to pieces, he shall bring it as evidence; he need not make restitution for the torn carcass.
If a man borrows an animal from his neighbor and it is injured or dies while its owner is not present, he must make full restitution.
If the owner was present, no restitution is required. If the animal was rented, the fee covers the loss.
If a man seduces a virgin who is not pledged in marriage and sleeps with her, he must pay the full dowry for her to be his wife.
If her father absolutely refuses to give her to him, the man still must pay an amount comparable to the bridal price of a virgin.
You must not allow a sorceress to live.
Whoever lies with an animal must surely be put to death.
If anyone sacrifices to any god other than the LORD alone, he must be set apart for destruction.

units.label: The foreigner, the widow, the orphan
units.note: I will surely hear their cry

verses.text: You must not exploit or oppress a foreign resident, for you yourselves were foreigners in the land of Egypt.
You must not mistreat any widow or orphan.
If you do mistreat them, and they {{cry|cry out}} to Me in distress, I will surely hear their cry.
My anger will be kindled, and I will kill you with the sword; then your wives will become widows and your children will be fatherless.
If you lend money to one of My people among you who is poor, you must not act as a creditor to him; you are not to charge him interest.
If you take your neighbor's cloak as collateral, return it to him by sunset,
because his cloak is the only covering he has for his body. What else will he sleep in? And if he cries out to Me, I will hear, for I am compassionate.

units.label: Holiness in ordinary obligations
units.note: you are to be My holy people

verses.text: You must not blaspheme God or curse the ruler of your people.
You must not hold back offerings from your granaries or vats. You are to give Me the firstborn of your sons.
You shall do likewise with your cattle and your sheep. Let them stay with their mothers for seven days, but on the eighth day you are to give them to Me.
You are to be My {{holy|holy}} people. You must not eat the meat of a mauled animal found in the field; you are to throw it to the dogs.

repay.gloss: To repay, restore, make whole, from the same root as shalom, peace. Restitution in Israel's law is never merely punitive; it is meant to restore what was broken and make the wronged party whole again.

cry.gloss: To cry out in distress, the same word used of Israel's own cry under Egyptian oppression, Exodus 3:7. God warns that the vulnerable person's cry reaches Him exactly as Israel's own cry once did, and He will act the same way in response.

holy.gloss: Holy, set apart, belonging distinctly to God. This closing command ties even the most mundane obligations, dietary rules, offerings, restitution, to Israel's fundamental identity as a people set apart to reflect God's own character.

basic.context: This chapter continues Israel's case law: how to make restitution for theft, how to settle disputes over borrowed or lost property, and firm penalties for serious sins. It closes with some of the most tender laws in the whole Torah, protecting foreigners, widows, orphans, and the poor from exploitation.
basic.hermeneutics: These are specific civil and case laws for ancient Israel's courts, not a direct legal code for governments today. Their enduring value is the moral principle underneath: restitution restores rather than merely punishes, and God cares fiercely for the powerless, principles fulfilled and deepened rather than abolished in the new covenant.
basic.originalLanguages: Restitution in this chapter is called shalam, to make whole, sharing a root with shalom, peace, restoring wholeness rather than merely inflicting pain. God warns that if the vulnerable tsa'aq, cry out, in distress, He will hear them exactly as He heard Israel's own cry in Egypt. And the whole chapter closes by calling Israel qadosh, holy, set apart in every corner of ordinary life.
basic.typology: The double restitution required of a thief anticipates the far greater restitution Christ makes, not merely restoring what was lost but giving abundantly more, life to the full, in the place of what sin destroyed.
basic.christ: Zacchaeus's spontaneous promise to repay fourfold anyone he had cheated, Luke 19:8, directly echoes this chapter's restitution laws, showing repentance in Christ working itself out in concrete, costly justice. And Jesus, who had no place to lay His head, embodies the same compassion this law commands toward the poor stranger with nothing to his name.
basic.god: God is meticulously just, insisting wrongs be made right down to the smallest detail of property and dispute, and He is fiercely compassionate, promising personal, furious intervention on behalf of anyone who exploits the widow, orphan, or foreigner. His justice and His tenderness toward the powerless are never separated.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 19:8-9 shows Zacchaeus applying this same principle of fourfold restitution after meeting Christ. James 1:27 defines pure religion as caring for orphans and widows, echoing this chapter directly. Deuteronomy 10:18-19 grounds Israel's care for the foreigner in the same memory of Egypt.
basic.oneStory: God builds justice and compassion into the smallest details of Israel's civil life, insisting that restitution restore what was broken and that the powerless never be exploited. This concern for the vulnerable and for genuine restitution runs through the whole Bible and finds its fullest expression in Christ, who restores far more than was ever lost.

context.literary: The chapter moves through escalating categories, property crimes, sexual and religious offenses, then softens into some of the Torah's most emotionally direct commands, protecting the powerless, before closing with a call to holiness that reframes every preceding law as an expression of covenant identity.
context.historical: These laws shaped how ancient Israelite courts handled everyday disputes over livestock, borrowed goods, and personal harm, in an agrarian society where such property represented a family's entire livelihood, and where the foreigner, widow, and orphan had no built-in legal protection apart from these commands.
context.canonical: The prophets repeatedly invoke this chapter's concern for the widow, orphan, and foreigner as the standard by which Israel's covenant faithfulness is measured, Isaiah 1:17, Zechariah 7:10, and the New Testament picks up the same concern as a mark of genuine faith, James 1:27.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish restitution, not mere punishment, as the shape of Israel's justice, and to root Israel's treatment of the powerless directly in their own memory of oppression in Egypt, so that mercy toward the vulnerable becomes inseparable from covenant faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes specific civil case law and social ethics for ancient Israel; it does not directly legislate modern criminal codes, banking regulations, or capital punishment for religious offenses. Its enduring moral principle, restitution that restores, protection for the powerless rooted in remembered suffering, and holiness reaching into ordinary economic life, is what carries forward as abiding truth, reissued under the ethics of the new covenant.

debates.question: How should the death penalty prescribed for sorcery, bestiality, and idolatry in verses 18 to 20 be understood today?

views.label: Civil law specific to Israel's theocratic nationhood
views.case: These offenses directly threatened Israel's covenant identity as a nation uniquely bound to the LORD; the civil penalty was tied to Israel's specific national calling and boundary-keeping, not a universal template for civil governments.

views.label: Reflecting the seriousness of the underlying sin, even where the civil penalty does not carry over
views.case: While the death penalty itself belonged to Israel's old covenant civil law and does not bind modern civil authorities or the church, the severity reflects how seriously God regards occult practice, idolatry, and sexual perversion, sins the New Testament continues to name gravely even without prescribing the same civil punishment, 1 Corinthians 6:9-11, Galatians 5:19-21.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the civil penalty was specific to Israel's covenant nationhood and does not directly apply today; the underlying moral seriousness of these sins, however, remains, addressed now through church discipline and gospel transformation rather than civil execution.

study.originalLanguages: Restitution here is shalam, making whole, sharing its root with shalom, peace, a justice oriented toward restoration, not mere retribution. God promises to hear the tsa'aq, the cry, of anyone oppressed, using the very word for Israel's own cry under Egyptian bondage, Exodus 3:7. And the whole chapter's civil detail is finally framed by the call to be qadosh, holy, set apart as belonging distinctly to God.

typology.type: Fourfold or double restitution for theft
typology.fulfillment: Echoed when Zacchaeus repays fourfold after meeting Christ, Luke 19:8, repentance expressed in concrete justice.

typology.type: God's fierce, personal defense of the widow, orphan, and foreigner
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's own ministry to the marginalized and in the church's call to pure religion, caring for orphans and widows, James 1:27.

study.christ: When Zacchaeus meets Jesus, his immediate response is to promise fourfold restitution to anyone he had cheated, Luke 19:8, a direct echo of this chapter's laws, showing that genuine encounter with Christ produces exactly this kind of concrete, costly justice. Christ Himself, who had nowhere to lay His head, embodies the compassion this law commands toward the poor and the stranger with nothing of their own.
study.god: God is exactingly just about small material disputes and blazingly protective of the powerless, promising to personally hear and avenge the cry of any widow, orphan, or foreigner who is mistreated. His compassion is not sentimental; He explicitly ties it to Israel's own memory of oppression, insisting that redeemed people never inflict on others what was once inflicted on them.

crossReferences.note: Zacchaeus's fourfold restitution after meeting Jesus directly echoes this chapter's restitution law.
Pure religion is defined as caring for orphans and widows, echoing this chapter's central concern.
Grounds Israel's care for the foreigner in the same remembered experience of Egypt invoked here.
The prophet indicts Israel for failing exactly this standard, defending the fatherless and pleading the widow's cause.

study.oneStory: God weaves justice and tender mercy into the smallest details of Israel's civil life, insisting that wrongs be made right and that the powerless never be exploited by those who were once powerless themselves. This same concern for restitution and for the vulnerable runs through the whole Bible and reaches its fullest expression in Christ, who restores immeasurably more than sin ever took.

quiz.q: What restitution was required if a man stole and slaughtered an ox, according to verse 1?

options.text: Five oxen for the one ox stolen.
options.note: The text specifies this heavier restitution because the stolen animal could no longer be returned alive.

options.text: A single ox in simple replacement.
options.note: The penalty for slaughtering or selling the stolen ox was heavier than simple replacement, five oxen.

options.text: No restitution was required if the ox was already dead.
options.note: Restitution was explicitly required, and at a multiplied rate for a slaughtered or sold animal.

options.text: The thief's own life was forfeit.
options.note: The penalty specified is restitution in livestock, not the death penalty, for simple theft of this kind.

quiz.q: What warning does God give about mistreating the widow, orphan, or foreigner in verses 22 to 24?

options.text: If they cry out to Him, He will surely hear, and His anger will be kindled against the oppressor.
options.note: This is God's exact, severe warning, tying His personal response to their cry.

options.text: God is indifferent to how the vulnerable are treated.
options.note: The text describes intense divine concern and threatened judgment, the opposite of indifference.

options.text: Only the community's human courts would punish such mistreatment.
options.note: The text frames God's own direct, personal response, not merely human legal process.

options.text: No warning is given regarding this behavior.
options.note: Verses 22 to 24 give an explicit and severe warning.

quiz.q: What restriction does verse 25 place on lending money to a poor Israelite?

options.text: No interest may be charged.
options.note: The text is explicit: you are not to charge him interest, protecting the poor from compounding debt.

options.text: Only very high interest could be charged.
options.note: The text forbids interest altogether in this case, not merely limits it to a high rate.

options.text: Loans to the poor were forbidden entirely.
options.note: Lending itself is assumed and even implicitly encouraged; only interest is forbidden.

options.text: No restriction is placed on lending terms.
options.note: Verse 25 explicitly restricts charging interest to the poor.

quiz.q: What must be done if a neighbor's cloak is taken as collateral, according to verses 26 to 27?

options.text: It must be returned by sunset, since it is his only covering for the night.
options.note: This law protects basic human dignity and physical need even amid a legitimate debt arrangement.

options.text: It may be kept indefinitely until the debt is paid in full.
options.note: The text requires prompt return by sunset regardless of whether the debt itself is settled.

options.text: It must be destroyed rather than returned.
options.note: The text calls for return, not destruction, of the pledged cloak.

options.text: No instruction is given about collateral.
options.note: Verses 26 to 27 give a specific and compassionate instruction about returning collateral.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament echo this chapter's concern for restitution and the vulnerable?

options.text: Zacchaeus promises fourfold restitution after meeting Jesus, and James defines pure religion as caring for orphans and widows.
options.note: Luke 19:8 and James 1:27 both directly echo this chapter's central concerns.

options.text: The New Testament shows no interest in restitution or care for the vulnerable.
options.note: Both Luke and James explicitly carry forward exactly these themes.

options.text: Paul explicitly overturns all concern for restitution as legalistic.
options.note: Paul does not reject restitution or care for the vulnerable; these remain consistent New Testament ethical concerns.

options.text: Only the Old Testament addresses these themes; they disappear afterward.
options.note: Luke 19:8 and James 1:27 show clear New Testament continuity with these themes.

questions: Israel's law required restitution that made the wronged party whole, not just punished the offender. When you have wronged someone, do you tend toward genuine restitution or merely feeling bad without concrete repair?

God's warning about the widow, orphan, and foreigner is startlingly personal, 'I will surely hear their cry.' Who in your community is currently crying out in a way you have not yet noticed or responded to?

The law forbade charging interest to a poor neighbor and required returning a pledged cloak by sunset. How does your own use of money reflect, or fail to reflect, this kind of practical compassion for those with less than you?
Zacchaeus responded to grace with immediate, costly restitution. Is there a debt of restitution, financial or relational, that grace is calling you to finally pay?
This whole chapter of civil law is framed by the call, 'you are to be My holy people.' Where do you compartmentalize your faith away from your ordinary financial dealings and everyday disputes?

translationNotes.note: The distinction between a thief killed at night versus after sunrise reflects an ancient legal principle about reasonable use of force in self-defense, presuming greater threat and less certainty of intent in darkness.

Sorceress is feminine in the Hebrew text, though the underlying concern, prohibiting occult practice that claimed power apart from or against the LORD, applied to men and women alike, as parallel laws elsewhere make clear, Deuteronomy 18:10-12.

The command to give the firstborn of sons echoes and assumes the redemption law of Exodus 13:11-15; sons were never to be sacrificed but always redeemed, unlike animals which were consecrated directly.
